1: Isaiah 15:1 begins the Moab oracle (massa Moav, the burden/oracle of Moab), one of the most emotionally affecting of the burden-oracles (Isaiah 13-23). 'Because Ar of Moab is destroyed in a single night, Moab is ruined; because Kir of Moab is destroyed in a night, Moab is ruined' — the double destruction of Moab's two major cities, repeated with the same framing. Ar (possibly modern Rabbah of Moab) and Kir (Kir-hareseth/Kir-haresh, modern Kerak) were the two chief Moabite cities. The Mesha Stele (c. 840 BCE, discovered 1868) is crucial background: this Moabite royal inscription records the same cities (Nebo, Medeba, Ataroth, Jahaz — all appearing in Isaiah 15) and describes Moabite victories over Israel in the 9th century, showing the deep history of Moabite national identity that the oracle sees destroyed.
2: 'The people go up to the temple and to Dibon, to the hilltop shrines to weep; they wail over Nebo and over Medeba — every head shaved bald and every beard cut off' — the mourning rites: going to the temple (beit, the house) and Dibon (the Moabite capital and major cult center, mentioned in the Mesha Stele as a place where Mesha built a high place for Chemosh). The hilltop shrines (bamot, the high places scattered across the land) are sites of lamentation. Nebo (Mount Nebo, where Moses died — Deuteronomy 34:1-5 — and the city of Nebo, a Moabite town) and Medeba (a town in the Moabite plateau, also mentioned in Mesha Stele) are the objects of mourning. The mourning rites: shaved heads (qorchah, bald-headness) and cut beards (gazuzah, clipped) were standard ANE mourning practices (Isaiah 22:12; Jeremiah 48:37; Micah 1:16) — deliberately disfiguring the body in grief.
3: 'In the streets they wear sackcloth; on the rooftops and in the public squares everyone mourns and weeps uncontrollably' — the pervasive mourning in every social space: in the streets (bechutsehotav) wearing sackcloth (saq, the rough burlap mourning garment — cf. Genesis 37:34, David mourning; 2 Kings 19:1, Hezekiah in sackcloth). On the rooftops (begagot) and in the public squares (birechovot) — the two spaces of Moabite daily social life, public and accessible, both filled with weeping. 'Everyone weeps uncontrollably' (yered bivechi, going down in weeping = dissolving into tears). The communal totality of the mourning — no private grief here but a whole nation's public lamentation — reflects the magnitude of the disaster.
4: 'Heshbon and Elealeh raise their cry — it can be heard all the way to Jahaz. At this even Moab's soldiers cry out; each one shudders within' — the sound of lamentation spreading across the Moabite plateau: from Heshbon (the major city of northern Moab, formerly Sihon's capital, Numbers 21:25-26) and Elealeh (modern el-Al, near Heshbon) the cry can be heard as far as Jahaz (a city in the Moabite plateau, Numbers 21:23 — the site where Israel defeated Sihon). The sound-geography traces the extent of the disaster by how far the lamentation can be heard. Even the halutzei-moav (the soldiers/armed men of Moab, those trained not to show emotion) cry out and 'each one shudders within' (nafesho yare'ah lo, his soul/life trembles within him).
5: 'My heart cries out for Moab. Her fugitives flee to Zoar, to Eglath-shelishiyah. At the climb up Luhith they go up in tears; on the Horonaim road they raise a desperate cry of ruin' — the prophet's compassion for Moab: 'my heart cries out for Moab' (libbi leMenua yezeaq, my heart cries out for Moab) — unprecedented prophetic empathy in a judgment oracle. Isaiah is not triumphant over Moab's fall but genuinely grieved. The refugee route traced: fleeing south to Zoar (the southernmost city, near the Dead Sea, where Lot fled from Sodom, Genesis 19:22-23), to Eglath-shelishiyah ('the third heifer,' probably a place name), up the ascent of Luhith, down the Horonaim road — tracing the desperate flight south along the King's Highway through Moab.
6: 'For the pools of Nimrim have dried up; the grass is withered, the new growth is gone, there is nothing green left' — the ecological dimensions of the judgment: the pools/waters of Nimrim (me Nimrim — probably near the Dead Sea in southern Moab) are dried up (ki me Nimrim lishamamot yihyu, for the waters of Nimrim will be desolations). The water source failure produces complete ecological collapse: the grass (deshe, the tender new grass) is withered, the vegetation (esev, herbage) is done (chalah, ended/finished), all green (yerakon, every green plant) is gone. The drying of water sources in a pastoral society is total economic collapse — livestock cannot survive, agriculture fails, human habitation becomes impossible. The ecological failure mirrors the social/military collapse described in Isaiah 15:1-5.
7: 'So whatever they have accumulated and saved they carry away across the Brook of the Willows' — the refugee economy: 'al-ken yitrah ashu vipiqudam, therefore the surplus they made and their stores they carry' (everything accumulated over generations) al-nachal ha'aravim yisaum (over the Brook of the Willows they carry them). The Brook of the Willows is probably the Wadi el-Hesa (ancient Zered, Numbers 21:12; Deuteronomy 2:13-14) — the southern boundary of Moab, crossing which means entering Edomite territory. The refugees carry their material wealth in flight — the savings of generations reduced to what can be physically transported by refugees fleeing south.
8: 'The sound of weeping has encircled the whole border of Moab — the wailing reaches Eglaim, the wailing reaches Beer-elim' — the geographic totality of lamentation: the wailing (ze'aqah, the cry/shriek) has surrounded (hiqqifah, encircled) Moab's entire border. The two endpoints of the border-circuit: Eglaim (in the south, possibly modern Rujm el-Gheilemeh) and Beer-elim (Well of the Mighty Ones, possibly near the northern border). The lamentation encircles the entire territory — there is no place within Moab's boundaries that has escaped the sound of mourning. The complete geographic perimeter of grief corresponds to the complete geographic scope of the disaster.
9: 'For the waters of Dimon run red with blood; and I will bring more upon Dimon — a lion against the fugitives of Moab and against what remains of the land' — the bloody conclusion to the Moab oracle: the waters of Dimon (probably the same as Dibon, with a wordplay — Dimon sounds like dam = blood in Hebrew, creating 'the waters of Bloodville run red with blood'). The intensification: 'I will add upon Dimon more' (ostif al-Dimon, I will add more upon Dimon) — a 'lion' (aryeh, the lion) against the fugitives and the remnant. The lion as YHWH's agent of final judgment against those who escaped the initial military defeat — no escape route is safe. The Moab oracle ends on this note of no-refuge, which makes the appeal to Zion in Isaiah 16:1-5 the only possible response.
